serving.description: 8 pieces of Yong Tau Foo with one bowl of Tom Yum soup
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of 8 pieces of Yong Tau Foo (approximately 280 g of stuffed items) served with one bowl of Tom Yum soup (approximately 120 ml broth), totalling approximately 400 g.

nutrient_source.title: Yong Tau Foo (mixed pieces with soup) — HPB Singapore Foodo / IMR Malaysia MyFCD composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR MyFCD)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB or MyFCD entry covers 8-piece Yong Tau Foo with Tom Yum soup as a combined dish. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing: (1) HPB Foodo 'Yong Tau Foo (mixed, with soup)' entry for the stuffed tofu pieces and clear soup baseline, and (2) IMR MyFCD Tom Yum soup broth values for the spiced broth component. Per-100g values reflect the blended dish at approximately 400 g total serving weight. Values are cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables for tofu and fish paste components. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed tofu and fish paste dish with soup — analogous estimate based on tofu-based mixed dish GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Yong Tau Foo with Tom Yum soup. GI estimated at 40 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for tofu-based mixed dishes (GI approximately 35–45) and the low-carbohydrate, high-protein, high-fibre composition of the dish. Tom Yum soup broth contributes negligible glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 20.8) / 100 = 8.3. Carbohydrate per serving estimated at 5.2 g/100g × 400 g = 20.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish paste, fish sauce, and soup broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium broth, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (approximately 1680 mg per full serving) approaches or may exceed the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40), high protein from fish paste and tofu, moderate dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Yong Tau Foo with Tom Yum soup is a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when ordered without noodles or rice, as the low GI (~40), high protein content (~5.8 g/100g), and moderate fibre support glycaemic control and satiety; glycaemic load per serving is approximately 8.3 (low).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium from broth and fish paste; low saturated fat and beneficial omega-3 from fish paste
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides a relatively low saturated fat content (~0.7 g/100g) and beneficial fish-derived protein, but the high sodium content (~420 mg/100g) warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing a smaller portion or requesting low-sodium broth is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yong Tau Foo with Tom Yum Soup (8 pieces) is a popular Malaysian-Singaporean dish of Hakka Chinese origin consisting of eight pieces of tofu, vegetables, and fish-paste-stuffed items served in a spicy and sour Tom Yum broth. The stuffed items typically include tofu puffs, bitter gourd, eggplant, chilli, okra, and fish balls, each filled with a seasoned fish paste mixture. The Tom Yum broth is prepared with lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, chilli, fish sauce, and lime juice, giving it a distinctively aromatic, spicy, and tangy flavour profile. The dish is minimally processed, with the fish paste items either steamed or lightly fried before being added to the soup. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (~68 kcal/100g), high in protein (~5.8 g/100g) from fish paste and tofu, low in saturated fat (~0.7 g/100g), and provides dietary fibre (~1.0 g/100g) from the vegetable components. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low), making it a suitable option for individuals managing blood glucose levels, particularly when consumed without noodles or rice. The primary clinical concern is the elevated sodium content (~420 mg/100g, approximately 1680 mg per full serving) from the fish paste, fish sauce, and soup broth, which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal-certified in most Singaporean and Malaysian hawker contexts and is dairy-free. It is a common hawker centre and food court meal in Singapore and Malaysia, frequently ordered as a light yet satisfying lunch or dinner option.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry covers 8-piece Yong Tau Foo with Tom Yum soup as a combined dish. Nutrient values are composited from HPB Singapore Foodo (Yong Tau Foo with soup) and IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Tom Yum soup broth), cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database tofu-based dish entries. Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation approach rather than a direct exact match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per-100g estimates for the combined dish (8 pieces of Yong Tau Foo + Tom Yum soup broth) at a total serving weight of approximately 400 g. 2. Sodium content is a key clinical concern; values reflect typical hawker-style preparation with fish sauce and fish paste. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as the dish contains no hydrogenated fats; fish paste and tofu are naturally free of industrial trans fats (confirmed zero per FAO/INFOODS SEA and HPB Foodo data). 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in HPB Foodo or IMR MyFCD for this dish category. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary composite sources for this dish. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary composite sources for this dish. 7. GI is estimated at 40 (low) based on analogous tofu-based mixed dish entries from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Yong Tau Foo with Tom Yum soup was found in the approved GI literature. 8. Halal status reflects typical hawker centre preparation; individual restaurant compliance should be verified. 9. The cuisine_tag 'Laksa' from the input source is not applicable to this dish and has been excluded. 10. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are estimated from composite HPB/IMR/FAO-INFOODS SEA data and carry medium confidence.
